question_results.question: What reason does the passage give for why some lies are told?
options: To avoid paying taxes
For the sake of social dignity or to keep a private secret
To gain political power
To improve athletic performance

question_results.question: According to the passage, how many times might a person hear lies each day?
options: 1 to 5 times
10 to 200 times
500 to 1000 times
None at all

question_results.question: Which combination of statements from the passage must be synthesized to justify the claim that most people are not naturally expert at detecting lies?
options: “Trained lie spotters get to the truth 90% of the time” and “The rest of us, we're only 54% accurate.”
“We lie more to strangers” and “Men lie eight times more about themselves.”
“Lying's a cooperative act” and “It's as old as breathing.”
“Babies will pretend to cry” and “Nine-year-olds, masters of covering up.”

question_results.question: The passage states “lying's a cooperative act.” What inference about communication does this most strongly support?
options: Lies are always imposed by one person onto another without consent.
Both liar and listener often participate implicitly, permitting lies to occur in social interactions.
Cooperation is unrelated to whether a lie succeeds.
Only professional liars can use lying cooperatively.

question_results.question: The passage lists examples from Dante, Shakespeare, and the Bible to support a point. What is the function of these examples in the author’s argument?
options: To claim those works invented lying in human history
To illustrate that deception has long been integrated into cultural narratives across history
To argue those authors were dishonest people
To show that lying is only a literary trope, not a real behavior

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument that lying has evolutionary value tied to intelligence?
options: Evidence showing deceitful behaviors are present equally across species regardless of cognitive ability.
A study finding trained lie spotters outperform untrained people.
Historical records showing literature often depicts lying.
Research confirming children at age five sometimes flatter others.

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the passage’s claim that we agreed to be lied to when we were lied to?
options: Social interactions often involve tacit expectations that prioritize politeness or dignity over blunt honesty.
Every lie is explicitly requested in advance by the listener.
Legal contracts require people to accept deception.
People cannot detect lies under any circumstances.

question_results.question: Which of the following best captures the main idea of the passage?
options: Lying is a modern phenomenon caused by digital technology and identity theft.
Lying is a pervasive, historically rooted human behavior that can be adaptive and socially functional.
Intelligence and lying are unrelated and lying decreases as species become smarter.
The passage argues that everyone should be trained to spot lies to eliminate deception.

question_results.question: Which group does the passage say is lied to more often: strangers or coworkers?
options: Coworkers
Strangers
Both equally
The passage does not compare them

question_results.question: Which paraphrased title would best suit the passage without using the same words from the text?
options: “Why Modern Technology Ended Truth Forever”
“The Ubiquity and Social Role of Deception in Human Life”
“A Scientific Proof That All People Are Dishonest”
“How to Never Be Fooled by Others”

question_results.question: Apply the passage’s claims to a new scenario: A company culture rewards politeness and “saving face” over blunt feedback. Which outcome does the passage’s logic most strongly predict?
options: Employees will never lie because corporate rules prohibit it.
Politeness norms will encourage everyday, cooperative lies that protect social dignity but may obscure real issues.
Employees will become unusually honest to rebel against the culture.
The company will immediately train everyone to be expert lie spotters.

question_results.question: At what age do children reportedly begin to “lie outright and try to control via flattery,” according to the passage?
options: One year old
Two years old
Five years old
Nine years old

question_results.question: Based on the passage’s tone and examples, what is the author’s overall attitude toward lying?
options: The author views lying as uniformly evil and without redeeming value.
The author thinks lying is an unavoidable, sometimes useful social practice woven into culture.
The author insists lying should be abolished immediately by law.
The author is indifferent and provides no evaluative stance.

question_results.question: What accuracy rate do trained lie spotters reportedly achieve?
If researchers say “the more intelligent the species, the more likely it is to lie,” which conclusion follows most reasonably from the passage combined with the child-development examples?
options: Lying appears early in development and may be linked to cognitive complexity that supports strategic social behavior.
Only adults with advanced education become frequent liars.
Intelligence causes lying directly through genetic mutation.
Less intelligent species never show any deceptive behavior.

question_results.question: How long has Mr. Torres been blind?
options: Since his daughter was born 13 years ago
Since he was 4 years old
Since five years ago when his dog was killed
Since he started working at the workshop

question_results.question: Which of the following best captures the author's overall tone toward Mr. Torres?
options: Respectful and admiring of his resilience
Mocking his inability to adapt to life
Indifferent to his personal struggles
Sympathetic but convinced he is irresponsible

question_results.question: Where does Alberto Torres live?
options: In a third-floor apartment in Brooklyn with no elevator
In a ground-floor apartment in Manhattan with an elevator
In a hospital-provided residence near the Bronx
In a suburb outside New York City

question_results.question: Which detail most strongly supports the idea that Mr. Torres values independence over pity?
options: He insists he is not acting out of the ordinary and avoids feeling like a victim.
He was given up to the care of the state at age 11.
His wife had a breast removed due to cancer.
He makes $20,000 a year.

question_results.question: What unstated assumption underlies the passage’s portrayal of Mr. Torres as admirable?
options: Overcoming repeated personal and practical hardships indicates moral strength and perseverance.
Anyone who works in a hospital must be inherently brave.
Blind people cannot hold any job that requires trust.
Financial compensation is the sole measure of job value.

question_results.question: If Mr. Torres’s commute were shortened by one hour each way, which likely consequence follows based on details in the passage?
options: He would spend less early-morning time preparing and could get more rest, potentially reducing the strain caused by long travel and the third-floor stairs.
He would no longer need his guide dog and could save money on its care.
He would immediately receive a higher salary because of reduced commute time.
He would be promoted to supervisor at the X-ray department.

question_results.question: Which combination of facts from the passage best explains why Mr. Torres continued working despite personal tragedies and low pay?
options: He believes feeling like a victim leads to bitterness; the hospital trusts and values his abilities; the job suits his tactile skill of working in the dark.
He wants to escape the hospital and return to the workshop; his wife’s cancer payments cover their expenses; he dislikes having a guide dog.
He hopes to become a bus driver eventually; he receives frequent promotions; he dislikes the long subway rides.
He is forced by law to work; he has no skills outside the workshop; the hospital pays significantly above average wages.

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most undermine the hospital director’s claim that “blind people can do just about anything except drive buses”?
options: A report showing blind technicians at the hospital frequently commit dangerous errors in X-ray development.
Evidence that some blind people successfully navigate public transit independently.
Testimony praising Mr. Torres’s accuracy and trustworthiness.
Data indicating blind employees prefer workshop jobs to hospital roles.

question_results.question: Why can't Mr. Torres wear gloves while working?
options: Because he needs to feel the film
Because the hospital forbids gloves in the dark room
Because gloves react with X-ray chemicals
Because gloves are too expensive for him

question_results.question: What is the most appropriate title for the passage?
options: A Blind Man’s Determination to Work and Live Independently
The History of X-ray Development in New York Hospitals
How Guide Dogs Cause More Harm Than Good
The Economic Policies Affecting Hospital Staff Wages

question_results.question: Suppose the hospital changed policy to require all X-ray technicians to wear gloves for safety. Which consequence would pose the strongest challenge to Mr. Torres continuing in his role?
options: He would be unable to perform the tactile tasks essential to developing film, making the requirement effectively incompatible with his method of working.
He would be slightly inconvenienced but could learn to work without feeling the film.
He would receive protective gloves designed for tactile sensitivity and continue unchanged.
The hospital would automate the task and hire him to manage the machines.

question_results.question: What time did the narrator find Mr. Torres at the subway stop?
options: 5 a.m.
7 a.m.
3 p.m.
Midnight

question_results.question: Based on context, what does the phrase "These are just little bumps you have to go over in your life" most nearly mean?
options: He regards hardships as manageable obstacles rather than insurmountable problems.
He believes life is full of painless and trivial events.
He thinks physical bumps cause most of his troubles.
He expects every difficulty to permanently block his progress.

question_results.question: Which inference about the hospital’s attitude toward disabled employees is best supported by the passage?
options: The hospital focuses on employees’ abilities and assigns tasks accordingly.
The hospital avoids hiring blind people for important roles.
The hospital believes blind people should only work in workshops.
The hospital requires all disabled staff to have supervisors watch them constantly.

question_results.question: What job does Mr. Torres hold at the hospital?
options: Developing X-ray film in the emergency room
Driving ambulances for emergency patients
Administering radiation treatments
Supervising the X-ray technicians

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the rule that the music player cannot see the players?
options: Being unseen prevents the music player from intentionally favoring or timing stops to benefit certain players
The music player is medically required to be blindfolded for safety

options: The game would necessarily end in one more round than before

question_results.question: What is the author's main purpose in this passage?
options: To criticize common party games and suggest alternatives
To explain how to play the game Musical Chairs clearly and simply
To argue that Musical Chairs is dangerous and should be banned
To recount a personal anecdote about winning a game

question_results.question: Suppose the music tempo is accidentally changed to a much faster speed mid-game. Based on the passage’s instructions, what is the most likely immediate effect on players’ movement?
options: Players will continue walking at the old speed regardless of the music
Players should begin walking more quickly to match the faster music
Players must stop walking because the rules prohibit any tempo change
Players will run to the nearest chair immediately and sit

question_results.question: Who is not supposed to see the players during the game?
options: The game referee
The person playing the music
The last remaining player
Any player who is out

options: If the music never stops, then a winner is chosen by the music player
If at each stop one player fails to find a chair and one chair is removed, then eventually one player will sit on the single remaining chair and win
If chairs are increased each round, then the last player standing loses
If players sit down before the music stops, then the game proceeds normally and the last sitter wins

question_results.question: Based on the description, what does the author imply is important for fair play?
options: The music player should be able to see and help the players
The music should match the players’ walking pace, being faster or slower accordingly
Players should be allowed to run when the music is fast
Players should change direction every time the music stops

options: The History and Origins of Musical Instruments
A Simple Guide to Playing Musical Chairs
The Psychology of Competitive Games
How to Build Chairs for Parties

question_results.question: According to the text, what must players do when the music starts?
options: Run to the nearest chair immediately
Walk round the chairs in time to the music
Sit down and wait for instructions
Stand still until the music stops

options: A drum set or a violin only
A piano, a tape recorder, or a radio
Only live instruments played by a person

question_results.question: Why must one chair be removed before the music starts again after someone is out?
options: To make the game last longer
To ensure there are fewer chairs than players so the process continues
To punish the person who dropped out
To give everyone a better chance to sit

question_results.question: How are the chairs recommended to be arranged in the passage?
options: In a circle with all chairs facing inward
In pairs back to back or in a single row with alternating directions
Scattered around the room randomly
Stacked on top of each other to save space

options: He becomes the new music player
He drops out of the game

options: No chairs were removed in earlier rounds

The players cheated to make the game end quickly

Random removal might cause chairs to be too far apart physically
Random removal could allow a player who was already out to return
Random removal could change which rounds produce immediate eliminations and introduce luck rather than consistent reduction tied to eliminations

distractor_results.question: What can be inferred about Yueyang Tower’s cultural status after the poems were written on its walls?
options: It became less known because the poems were unpopular.
It achieved greater fame and cultural prominence.
It was converted into a military fortress permanently.
It lost its architectural uniqueness immediately.
distractor_results.reason: A: PASS. Uses keywords "less known" and "poems" but creates a false statement that contradicts the text's claim of becoming better known. C: PASS. Uses "military fortress," a partial truth from the tower's origin, but falsely claims it was converted permanently after the poems. D: PASS. Uses "architectural uniqueness," a topic mentioned in the text, but falsely claims it was lost immediately.

distractor_results.question: Suppose a preservationist claims that the tower’s unusual helmet-roof is the main reason visitors come today. Which detail from the passage would most weaken that claim?
options: The tower has three stories.
Fan Zhongyan’s famous poem made the tower better known than before.
The tower lies near Dongting Lake.
There is a tomb of Xiaoqiao in the garden north of the tower.
distractor_results.reason: A: PASS. Misapplies the text's logic by offering a neutral architectural fact that doesn't actually weaken a claim about the roof's popularity. C: PASS. Presents a plausible real-world reason for visiting (scenery) not directly used to weaken the literature vs. architecture argument in the text's logic. D: PASS. distractor_results.question: If a historian wanted to argue that Yueyang Tower’s fame is due more to literature than to architecture, which two facts from the passage would best support that claim?
options: The tower’s height is 21.35 meters and it has three stories.
Poems by famous poets were written on its walls, and Fan Zhongyan’s well-known poem made the tower better known.
Lu Su trained soldiers there and the platform served as a navy training ground.
The Huaifu Pavilion honors Du Fu and Xiaoqiao’s tomb lies north of the tower.
distractor_results.reason: A: PASS. Sophisticated trap that uses specific architectural data from the text to support the opposite of the intended claim (architecture instead of literature). C: PASS. Reverses the logic by focusing on military history, which is a disjointed fact used to distract from the literary argument. D: PASS. Presents a "partial truth" by mentioning the Huaifu Pavilion (literary) but pairs it with Xiaoqiao’s tomb (personal/historical), which is less direct support than B.

distractor_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the author’s mention that Fan Zhongyan’s words “made the tower even better known than before”?
options: Literary fame can increase public recognition of a physical landmark.
Military use of a site always diminishes cultural value.
Architectural uniqueness alone guarantees lasting fame.
Tombs nearby are the primary reason for a tower’s historical importance.
distractor_results.reason: B: PASS. Misinterprets the text's logic by creating a false dichotomy between military use and cultural value not stated in the passage. C: PASS. A plausible real-world assumption (uniqueness equals fame) that the author actually subverts by emphasizing the poem's impact. D: PASS. A sophisticated trap that takes a disjointed fact (tombs) and incorrectly identifies it as a "primary" cause of importance.

question_results.question: Which of the following most accurately paraphrases the passage's claim about employment among qualified Americans with disabilities?
options: A majority are unemployed or underemployed, with many living at or below poverty level.
Almost all are employed in high-paying jobs.
Employment rates for disabled people exceed those of the general population.
Disabled people rarely seek employment by choice.

question_results.question: Which choice best captures the author's primary purpose in this passage?
options: To argue that medical advances have fully solved problems facing people with disabilities
To highlight ongoing social and structural barriers that prevent disabled people from full participation despite legal progress
To provide a detailed medical history of genetic disabilities
To advocate for segregation of people with disabilities for their protection

question_results.question: In the passage, what civil liberties were historically denied to people with disabilities?
options: Right to vote, marry, get an education, and gain employment
Right to free medical care only
Right to drive and own property
Right to travel internationally

question_results.question: Which additional fact, if true, would most strengthen the author's claim that public attitudes must advance for full inclusion?
options: Surveys show that accessible laws exist but large percentages of the public hold discriminatory beliefs toward disabled people.
There is a sudden medical breakthrough that cures most disabilities.
Most public buildings already comply fully with accessibility standards.
Employment rates for disabled people are higher than the national average.

question_results.question: Which of the following basic needs does the passage say advancements in medicine and social services have made attainable in principle for people with disabilities?
options: Luxury housing
Food, shelter, and medical treatment
Private transportation
High-paying employment

question_results.question: Based on the passage, which title would best fit the entire text?
options: The Medical Causes of Developmental Disabilities
Progress and Remaining Barriers for People with Disabilities
How to Cure Disabilities: A Modern Guide
Economic Policies That Harm Disabled People

question_results.question: The term "adventitious" in the passage most nearly means:
options: Present from birth
Accidental or caused by outside forces
Mild and temporary
Genetically inherited

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the author's argument that transportation barriers limit social participation for disabled people?
options: That access to transportation is necessary for engaging in many community and social activities.
That people with disabilities prefer to remain at home regardless of transportation options.
That transportation is the only factor determining social inclusion.
That public laws ban people with disabilities from social events.

question_results.question: Applying the passage's discussion to a new scenario: A company has hired many qualified disabled employees but has not modified its workplace and few coworkers interact with them. Which conclusion best follows from the passage?
options: Legal protection and employment alone do not ensure meaningful inclusion; structural accommodations and attitude change are also needed.
Hiring is sufficient; no further changes are necessary for full participation.
Disabled employees should be reassigned to remote work only.
The company has solved all barriers because jobs were provided.

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the passage's suggestion that legal change has outpaced public attitudes?
options: A large-scale public education campaign led to a measurable decline in discriminatory behaviors after the laws passed.
Laws protecting disabled people were never actually enacted.
Medical treatments eliminated most disabilities before laws were passed.
Transportation remains universally inaccessible everywhere.

question_results.question: Suppose a city eliminates all transportation barriers but public awareness remains low. According to the passage's logic, what is the most likely outcome for disabled residents' social inclusion?
options: They may still be excluded socially because laws alone do not guarantee acceptance.
They will automatically achieve full social integration.
They will be legally barred from social activities despite improved transit.
They will no longer need any medical or social services.

question_results.question: According to the passage, approximately how many Americans have physical, mental, or other disabilities?
options: Over 35 million
About 5 million
Fewer than 10 million
Exactly 50 million

question_results.question: The phrase "effectively excluded" in the passage most likely implies:
options: Legally prevented from participating
Practically unable to participate because of real-world obstacles
Fully welcomed into community activities
Physically restrained by law

question_results.question: Which statement is a reasonable inference about public attitudes, based on the passage's final sentence?
options: Laws alone are insufficient; societal attitudes must improve for full inclusion.
Public attitudes have already surpassed legal protections.
Only legal changes, not attitudes, matter for disabled people's inclusion.
Attitudes are irrelevant if transportation is provided.

question_results.question: What does the passage call disabilities that occur before an individual's twenty-second birthday and affect multiple areas of development?
options: Adventitious
Developmental
Temporary
Acquired

That whales cannot survive in warm water regions

question_results.question: Which choice best identifies a subtle logical difference between the authors' attitudes toward "the present"?
options: Passage 1 treats presence as a skill to cultivate repeatedly; Passage 2 treats it as an occasional, involuntary event best tolerated rather than pursued.
Both passages maintain identical prescriptions: practice being present hour after hour without exception.
Passage 1 argues the present is harmful; Passage 2 argues it is the only source of joy.
Both authors assert that planning for diplomas is the supreme purpose of life.

question_results.question: What unstated assumption connects the two passages about attention to the present?
options: That being mindful always leads to external rewards such as diplomas.
That attention to the present competes with attention to memory and anticipation.
That the present is an illusion with no real effects on emotions.
That concerts are the only place the present can intrude.

question_results.question: Which paraphrased statement best reflects the main implicit message of Passage 1 (must not use original phrasing)?
options: We should postpone happiness until we achieve long-term goals.
Everyday life offers immediate opportunities for contentment if we intentionally notice them.
Only travel and big events can produce true joy.
Present experiences are irrelevant compared to future plans.

options: Travel far away

Change our neighborhood

options: "The Necessity of Constant Mindfulness"
"Two Views on Living in the Present"
"How to Never Think About the Past"
"The Science of Time Management"

question_results.question: Which statement best captures the contrast between the two passages?
options: Both passages equally celebrate constant attention to the present moment.

Passage 1 favors brooding about the future; Passage 2 praises meditation.
Both passages argue that planning is more important than living.

question_results.question: Which inference about the narrator of Passage 2 is most supported by the text (paraphrased)?
options: The narrator always rejects any experience that interrupts memory.
The narrator tolerates occasional awareness of now but prefers reflection on other times.
The narrator is a strict proponent of living solely in the present moment.
The narrator finds live music unbearable under all circumstances.

options: Many people lack basic needs and cannot focus on mindful appreciation.

Some concerts momentarily force listeners into present awareness.
Occasional interruptions from the present are inevitable.

Obsessive future planning is the only productive strategy for success and happiness.

question_results.question: How many applications did the narrator fill out before getting one offer?

Twenty-five
Fifty
One hundred

question_results.question: What grade was the narrator teaching when she described having seventeen boys and five girls?

Sixth grade

Ninth grade

question_results.question: Apply the principle “respecting the pupil” from the passage to a hypothetical new teacher who faces frequent student interruptions: which action best embodies that principle while addressing the disruptions?
options: Publicly reprimand every student who interrupts to discourage others.
Dismiss interruptions as unimportant and continue lecturing.
Privately discuss with disruptive students to understand motives and involve them in shaping class activities.
Assign additional homework to punish the whole class for interruptions.

question_results.question: What does the principal’s suggestion to “get to the meat of literature and stop talking about symbolism” most likely imply about the narrator’s teaching style before the feedback?
options: She focused too much on abstract literary concepts that failed to engage her students.
She spent excessive time on classroom discipline and ignored literature.
She emphasized practical writing exercises over reading.
She used hands-on activities and dramatizations for every story.

question_results.question: Which famous writer's idea is quoted at the end of the passage about respecting the pupil?
options: Thoreau
Emerson
Whitman
Dickinson

question_results.question: Which of the following most weakens the narrator’s claim that the principal “made a teacher” of her by teaching her Emerson’s idea?
options: Evidence that other teachers improved without a mentor’s help suggests mentorship is unnecessary.
A report showing the narrator’s students improved only after she adopted stricter punishments absent any philosophical change.
A letter from the principal claiming he never discussed Emerson with the narrator.
A study proving that respect for pupils is unrelated to classroom management.

question_results.question: Based on the passage, what is the most likely reason the narrator initially ignored student misbehavior?

She wanted to appear weak to gain their sympathy.

question_results.question: What inference can be drawn about why the narrator still drives the same winding road fifteen years later?
options: She prefers long commutes over living near the school.
The support she received in her first year helped her build a lasting career and attachment to the school.
She was unable to find any other job offers after the first year.
She disliked urban life and wanted to stay in a rural area regardless of her job.

Rural schools are inherently unsuitable for young teachers from cities.
Mentorship is unnecessary if a teacher has strong optimism from the start.

question_results.question: Which hypothetical finding would most weaken the passage’s main claim that women invented agriculture and sparked civilization?
options: Evidence that men also participated in early seed planting around the same time and place.

question_results.question: Which combination of statements from the passage must be synthesized to conclude that men began civilization after women invented agriculture?
options: Men invented machines and guns; women dropped seeds; hunters built cities.

Archaeologists say women invented things; men were cleverer; cities appeared spontaneously.
Women liked animals; men invented the alphabet; agriculture was discovered by accident.

question_results.question: What is the author’s main purpose in this passage?
options: To list all inventions by men throughout history
To argue that women played a crucial role in the development of civilization by inventing agriculture
To explain how rockets and guns changed society
To show that hunting was safer than farming

question_results.question: Applying the passage’s logic to a new scenario: if another community invents a reliable energy source that allows people to stay home rather than travel for work, what parallel outcome does the passage suggest might occur?

The community would return to nomadic lifestyles immediately.

options: Men continued to hunt more because agriculture failed.
Women left home to hunt while men stayed to farm.
Men could stay home and build settlements while women cared for children and animals.
Both men and women became nomadic to follow wild animals.

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the author’s argument that women’s invention of agriculture led to civilization?
options: Settled farming necessarily allows for the development of permanent communities.
Inventing tools like rockets is more important than farming for social development.
Hunting always leads to the extinction of animal species.
Cities form only where there are nearby oceans.

options: Men
Scientists
Women
Archaeologists

options: In Europe
In the Middle East
In North America
In Asia

options: Metal tools
Roots, fruit and grass
Wheat and barley
Livestock

options: The invention of agriculture made hunting more dangerous than before.

distractor_results.question: Which inference about social roles after agriculture is most supported by the passage?
options: Men continued to hunt more because agriculture failed.
Women left home to hunt while men stayed to farm.
Men could stay home and build settlements while women cared for children and animals.
Both men and women became nomadic to follow wild animals.
distractor_results.reason: A: PASS. Uses keywords "men" and "hunt" but creates a statement that contradicts the text’s claim about the shift to settlements. B: PASS. Highly deceptive as it uses "keywords" like "home" and "hunt" but swaps the gender roles described in the text. D: PASS. Uses the concept of "wild animals" but presents a "partial truth" regarding movement that is ultimately the opposite of the "stay at home" conclusion.

distractor_results.question: Which of the following best describes the author’s attitude toward the common claim “women never invented things”?
options: The author agrees and provides additional supporting examples.
The author challenges the claim and presents counterevidence.
The author ignores the claim completely.
The author suggests the claim is a modern myth with no historical relevance.
distractor_results.reason: A: PASS. Deceptive because the author *does* agree men invented many things, but fails the second half of the statement, making it a "partial truth." C: PASS. Uses sophisticated language ("modern myth") to frame a false interpretation of the author's historical focus.

question_results.question: Which of the following would be the best alternative title for the passage?

The Mischievous Adventures of a Curious Little Boy
How to Discipline a Problematic Child
The History of a Small Town’s Neighborhood Disputes

question_results.question: What did Daniel think needed washing that led Angela to find him near the washing machine?
options: The baby’s clothes
Jasper the dog
The neighbor’s doll
His own shoes

question_results.question: Suppose the Browns install safety locks and keep the washing machine locked; which likely outcome follows from the text’s description of Daniel?
options: Daniel will stop being curious and become uninterested in exploring.
Daniel will find other risky ways to satisfy his curiosity, causing different troubles.
Daniel will immediately become perfectly obedient at home.
The baby sister Laura will become the naughtiest child instead.

question_results.question: What tone does the narrator most likely use when describing Daniel’s behavior?
options: Harsh and condemning, emphasizing he is irredeemably bad
Amused and bemused, highlighting mischief with affection
Academic and clinical, focusing on psychological diagnosis
Angry and vengeful toward the parents

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken Angela’s hope that Daniel will improve with age?
options: Daniel shows similar curiosity-related behavior at school but is well behaved there.
Multiple caregivers report that Daniel’s risky behaviors have increased over time.
Daniel enjoys reading books and giving them as gifts.
Daniel’s parents consistently supervise him and limit his access to water.

question_results.question: Which sentence best captures the author’s implicit view of Daniel’s actions?
options: Daniel is malicious and intends to harm others for fun.
Daniel’s misbehavior stems from curiosity rather than deliberate cruelty.
Daniel is model student behavior and never causes trouble.
Daniel’s actions are carefully planned and maliciously executed.

question_results.question: If a psychologist were to argue that Daniel’s actions are primarily attention-seeking, which two facts from the passage would best support that claim?
options: He is well behaved at school and has golden hair.
He gives his mother a book and smiles after a dangerous act.
He floods the kitchen when the baby cries and pulls a wardrobe over after curiously exploring.
The neighbors stopped speaking to the family and the carpet was replaced.

question_results.question: What unstated assumption underlies Angela’s statement "I hope things will get better as he gets older"?
options: Children’s destructive behavior naturally decreases with maturity and better judgment.
Neighbors will forgive the family over time regardless of future incidents.
Replacing the carpet will prevent further damage to the house.
Daniel’s curiosity derives from a permanent character defect that cannot change.

the book included official government documents.

question_results.question: What does the passage imply about Selkirk’s character before he was left on the island?
options: He had a temper and fled to sea after trouble in church.
He was a celebrated church leader.
He was a successful merchant in England.
He was a quiet and obedient crew member.

options: The author blends factual incidents with fictional elaboration to craft believable narrative.

question_results.question: Which combination of facts from the passage explains why Robinson Crusoe had widespread influence on later English novels?
options: Defoe’s vivid survival detail + the novel’s acceptance as realistic = influential model for characters in later fiction.

question_results.question: What detail about Defoe's work habits is stated in the passage?
options: He wrote a whole newspaper three times a week.

question_results.question: Which choice, if true, would most weaken the passage’s claim that Robinson Crusoe was inspired by Selkirk’s story?

Changing size automatically reduces production costs to zero.

Its size change automatically makes it the best-selling paper.

question_results.question: If a critic argued that Webster’s spelling changes weakened ties between American and British English, which part of the passage would most directly contradict that claim?
options: The mention that Webster added local words to the dictionary
The statement that linguists believed his dictionary helped a new nation achieve unity and cultural independence
The detail about Webster’s definition of "preposterous"
The fact that Webster was born in 1758

question_results.question: What change did Webster make to the word "centre" for teaching children?
options: He changed it to "centur."
He removed the "r" at the end.
He changed it to "center."
He added an extra "e."

question_results.question: Which combination of facts from the passage best supports the claim that Webster’s work had cultural significance beyond mere spelling changes?
options: He celebrated his 250th birthday in 2008 and wrote controversial definitions.
He spent 28 years compiling a 70,000-word dictionary that used American spellings and was widely used in schools, and linguists said it helped achieve unity and cultural independence.
He was born in 1758 and changed "centre" to "center."
Joshua Kendall is writing a biography and Webster included local words.

question_results.question: Which phrase best captures the author’s attitude toward Webster’s influence on American spelling?
options: The author views Webster as an unnecessary meddler in language.
The author portrays Webster as a key figure who strengthened American identity through language.
The author believes Webster’s changes were purely accidental and unimportant.
The author thinks Webster’s dictionary harmed American education.

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the author’s claim that Webster “helped a new nation achieve unity and cultural independence” through his dictionary?
options: That a standardized form of language can contribute to a sense of shared national identity
That political freedom is unrelated to cultural matters
That dictionaries have no influence on education
That Webster’s personal opinions were the main reason for political unity

question_results.question: Who created the American-style spellings such as dropping the "u" in "colour"?
options: Joshua Kendall

A group of linguists
British teachers

options: It would likely promote a single global political system.
It could foster a sense of shared identity among speakers who adopt the new spellings and distinguish them from others.
It would eliminate all dialectal differences immediately.
It would make older dictionaries more popular.

question_results.question: In what year was Webster's 70,000-word dictionary completed?

options: The Influence of an American Lexicographer on National Language and Identity
British Spelling Traditions and Their Unchanged Dominance
A Complete Biography of Joshua Kendall
The Decline of English Language Study in Schools

question_results.question: Based on the passage, what is the most likely reason Webster removed the British "u" from some words?
options: To make words match pronunciation and promote a distinct American identity
To comply with British spelling reforms after the war
To make words longer and more complex for learners
To confuse readers with inconsistent rules

options: The History of Camping in America
The Daily Routine of Working Men

How to Cook and Clean Efficiently

question_results.question: Which of the following best combines two ideas from different parts of the text to explain why Mother's Camp is attractive?
options: Because husbands always refuse to cook and children never ask questions.
Because many women work full-time and still do most of the housework, they welcome a place where they neither cook nor clean for a while.

question_results.question: Which inference is best supported by the passage about chocolate shops in London?

question_results.question: If it is true that one third of the world’s population starts the day with the "wide-awake" feeling, which conclusion follows most reasonably from the passage’s information about coffee?
options: Coffee consumption has become globally widespread and influential since its discovery and spread.
Coffee remains only a regional specialty limited to Ethiopia and Brazil.
Coffee was never popular outside the Arab world.
Coffee was first discovered in South America.

question_results.question: Apply the passage’s pattern to a hypothetical: If a nutritious grain native to Asia were brought to South America in the 1600s and later became a staple there, which multi-step consequence would best mirror those described in the passage?
options: The grain would remain unknown outside a small village and have no broader impact.
The grain could become widely cultivated in South America, influence diets and economies there, and potentially cause social upheaval if crops failed or dependency developed.
The grain’s origin would be forgotten immediately, and it would be credited to South America alone without any cultural exchange.
The grain would only be used for ceremonial purposes and never as food.

question_results.question: Which statement best reflects the author’s attitude toward the historical movement of foods?
options: The author treats the movement as insignificant and uninteresting.
The author views the exchange of foods as one of the most dramatic changes in people’s lives over the last 500 years.
The author believes only European foods influenced the rest of the world.
The author blames native peoples for spreading crops worldwide.

To criticize the Spanish for introducing foreign crops to Europe

The gardener secretly hates roses and wants them gone.
The gardener is unaware roses exist in his garden.

This presents a plausible real-world assumption (hating the item) which is not supported by the specific "surplus" logic established in the text. Distractor D: PASS. This is a logical flaw/contradiction (he can't discard what he doesn't know exists), serving as a sophisticated trap.

question_results.question: Which of the following best captures the author's primary purpose in the passage?
options: To warn readers about the global scale and health consequences of smoking
To provide a historical account of tobacco cultivation techniques
To compare smoking rates between teenagers and adults
To advertise products that help people quit smoking

question_results.question: If you had to give a concise title to this passage, which is most appropriate?
options: "Global Health Threat: The Deadly Impact of Smoking"
"A History of Cigarette Manufacturing"
"Sports and Smoking: A Growing Trend"
"Cooking Methods Involving Tobacco Leaves"

question_results.question: Which piece of additional information, if true, would most weaken the passage's argument that quitting reduces smoking-related deaths?
options: A large, long-term study finds that former heavy smokers who quit have the same long-term mortality rates from smoking-related diseases as those who never quit.
A new survey shows some smokers prefer e-cigarettes over traditional cigarettes.
A report documents rising cigarette sales in one country.
A cosmetic study shows quitting improves skin appearance.

question_results.question: What percent of smoking deaths worldwide occur in Russia and Eastern Europe and similar areas, according to the passage?
The passage states smoking is known to cause which disease?
options: Lung cancer
Diabetes
Alzheimer's disease
Measles

question_results.question: What does the statement "Scientists say smoking will kill 50% of the smokers" imply about long-term risk?
options: Half of all people who smoke are expected to die because of smoking
Smokers live twice as long as non-smokers
Smoking prevents half of all lethal diseases
Only those who smoke more than 15 cigarettes daily are at risk

question_results.question: Apply the passage's information to a hypothetical: If smoking causes both lung and mouth cancers and increases heart disease risk, which combined public-health outcome is most plausible if a population's smoking rate halves?
options: Incidence of lung and mouth cancers and smoking-related heart disease would likely decrease, reducing overall mortality linked to smoking.
Incidence of those diseases would double immediately.
Reducing smoking would have no effect because smoking causes none of these diseases.
Heart disease would disappear completely, but cancers would remain unchanged.

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the claim that stopping smoking reduces the chance of dying from smoking?
options: The harmful effects of smoking are at least partly reversible or their progression can be halted when smoking ceases.
Stopping smoking immediately eliminates all other health risks unrelated to tobacco.
Only medical treatments, not quitting, can improve the health of smokers.
People who quit smoking will never again be at risk of any disease.

question_results.question: Based on the passage, which inference about gender and smoking is most supported?
options: Historically, men have accounted for more smoking deaths than women, especially in developing countries.
Women have always suffered more smoking deaths than men worldwide.
Smoking deaths have been evenly split between men and women in developing countries.
The passage claims only women in developed countries smoke heavily.

question_results.question: According to the study, how many people die each year worldwide from smoking at the time of the report?
options: 3 million
6 million
10 million
1 million

question_results.question: Suppose a country has 100 million adult men and follows the global pattern stated in the passage for China and Latin America combined (70% smoke like China or 50% like Latin America). If policymakers claim targeting men will reduce most smoking deaths, which reasoning best supports that claim based on the passage?
options: Because a very large proportion of men in several regions are smokers and most smoking deaths so far have been among men, focusing interventions on men could substantially lower overall deaths.
Because children are the main group of smokers, targeting men will have no effect.
Because smoking affects only women, targeting men is irrelevant.
Because smoking prevalence is uniformly low, targeting any group is unnecessary.

question_results.question: According to experts in the passage, what happens to smokers who stop smoking completely?
options: They improve their health
They immediately gain immunity to lung cancer
They become more likely to die from heart disease
They must continue low-dose smoking to reduce risk

question_results.question: Which of the following best identifies a potential limitation in the passage's projection that deaths could rise to 10 million by 2020?
options: The projection assumes current smoking rates continue unchanged and does not account for future policy changes, cessation programs, or medical advances that could alter mortality.
The projection is invalid because smoking has never caused any deaths.
The passage provides precise local smoking laws that guarantee the projection will occur.
The number 10 million is cited as an absolute certainty with no underlying assumptions.

question_results.question: Which option most accurately paraphrases the passage's claim about trends if smoking rates continue?
options: If current smoking habits persist, annual smoking deaths could increase to about ten million by 2020.
Smoking deaths are predicted to drop below one million by 2020.
Smoking will be completely eradicated worldwide by 2020.
Smoking-related deaths will remain constant indefinitely.

options: China
Russia
Latin America
Eastern Europe

question_results.question: According to the text, what is happening to tobacco vending machines in England today?
options: They will be required to display health warnings.
They will be officially banned from selling tobacco.
They will be subject to increased taxation.
They will only sell filtered cigarettes.

question_results.question: Which scenario, if true, would most weaken the passage's claim that vending machine bans will protect children?
options: A study shows 11% of young smokers used vending machines.
Retailers uniformly remove all tobacco advertising from their shops.
Most underage smokers obtain cigarettes from social sources (friends/family) rather than vending machines.
Plain packaging is implemented nationally and reduces marketing appeal.

question_results.question: What inference can be made about why small shops were given until April 2015 to remove tobacco displays?
options: Small shops were considered a greater health risk than large retailers.
Small shops likely needed more time to comply due to resource or logistical constraints.
The government planned to exempt small shops permanently.
Small shops had already removed displays before the regulation.

question_results.question: Consider this policy proposal: introduce strict ID card scanners on all remaining tobacco outlets but keep vending machines banned. Based on the text's claims, which predicted outcome is most consistent with the author's perspective?
options: Youth smoking will likely increase because scanners make buying easier.
Such measures would likely complement vending machine bans and further reduce youth access and appeal.
ID scanners would replace plain packaging as the only effective measure.
The tobacco industry would support ID scanners as increasing their profits.

question_results.question: Which statement best summarizes the author's main purpose in this passage?
options: To argue that vending machines are the only source of cigarettes for young smokers.
To inform readers about recent and upcoming UK measures aimed at reducing youth smoking.
To show how tobacco companies have improved age verification on vending machines.
To promote vending machines as a safe way to distribute tobacco.

question_results.question: Who is quoted expressing concern about children getting tobacco from vending machines?
options: The UK Health Secretary
A tobacco industry spokesperson

options: Scotland and England only
Wales and Northern Ireland
Ireland and Wales
Scotland and Northern Ireland

question_results.question: What is the primary reason the tobacco industry is described as "fighting tooth and nail" in the passage?
options: To reduce cigarette prices across the UK
To prevent changes that would reduce their profits by losing young smokers
To support the government's public consultation
To encourage plain packaging proposals

question_results.question: Which assumption underlies the argument that banning vending machines will help reduce youth smoking?
options: Young people primarily obtain cigarettes through online marketplaces.
Vending machines do not check customers' ages, making them a significant source for underage smokers.
Tobacco advertising never influences young people's choices.
Removing vending machines will automatically end all tobacco use among youth.

question_results.question: Which of the following, taken together from the text, provides the strongest rationale for the UK government's consultation on plain packaging?
options: High numbers of young people start smoking annually and vending machines supply some underage smokers; plain packaging could make cigarettes less appealing and improve warnings.
Tobacco companies are increasing profits and want vending machines to remain; thus, consultations always favor industry.
Vending machines are already banned everywhere in Europe, so plain packaging is the next step.
Small shops will never remove displays, so packaging is the only option.

April 2014
April 2015
April 2012

question_results.question: Based on the passage, which effect would plain packaging most likely have if introduced?
options: It would increase the marketing appeal of cigarettes to young people.
It would make health warnings less prominent.
It would reduce the attractiveness of cigarettes to young people and enhance warnings.
It would legalize vending machines again across Europe.

Encourage tobacco companies to market cigarettes directly to young adults.
Allow vending machines with stronger advertising to educate youth about smoking.

options: An immediate rise in national academic reputation without other changes

Updated logos and marketing materials
A modest increase in applications due to improved image

question_results.question: Which combination of statements best captures the passage’s overall message about college name changes?
options: Name changes are cosmetic and have no measurable effects; marketing in higher education is unnecessary.
Colleges rename themselves to project new roles or break past associations; when combined with substantive changes, rebranding can correlate with improved outcomes.
Every college that renames itself will become a university and see immediate academic gains.

options: Marketing has become more accepted and influential in higher education decisions

The university created new business cards and banners

options: A college president advocating for higher tuition

options: A decrease in undergraduate enrollment

question_results.question: Which term from the passage refers to microscopic organisms in the gut?
options: Endotoxin
Microbiota
Bacterium

question_results.question: What was one health improvement mentioned besides weight loss?

question_results.question: Based on the passage, which unstated assumption underlies the researchers' treatment approach for the obese patient?
options: Reducing endotoxin-producing gut bacteria will lead to weight loss and improved liver condition.
All obesity cases are caused by the same bacterial strain identified in this patient.
Increasing overall calorie intake is necessary to change gut microbiota composition.
Surgical removal of gut bacteria is the most effective treatment.

question_results.question: Which choice best captures the author's primary claim about causes of obesity in the passage?
options: Obesity is solely caused by holiday overeating and lack of exercise.
Certain gut bacteria, rather than just overeating, can contribute to obesity.
Genetics are the only determinant of obesity.
Modern diets have no effect on body weight.

question_results.question: What did the researchers feed the obese patient to reduce gut bacteria?
options: Low-calorie solid meals
Specialized nutritional liquid food
Probiotic supplements
High-protein shakes

question_results.question: How much weight did the patient lose after 23 weeks of treatment?
options: 23 kg
35 kg
175 kg

question_results.question: If researchers wanted to strengthen the causal claim that the specific bacterium causes obesity, which study design change would best address Lita Proctor's concern mentioned in the passage?
options: Conduct a controlled experiment where subjects are randomly assigned to have their endotoxin-producing bacteria reduced and then compare weight changes to a placebo group.
Survey more holiday diners about their turkey and hot chocolate consumption and correlate with weight.
Measure endotoxin levels only after patients have lost weight to document association.
Rely solely on mouse studies from 2004 without human clinical trials.

question_results.question: Suppose a different obese patient had 10 percent of gut bacteria producing endotoxin and did not respond to the same liquid diet. Which explanation best aligns with the passage's information and logic?
options: Multiple factors besides endotoxin-producing bacteria contribute to obesity, so lowering those bacteria may not be sufficient for everyone.
The passage proves that endotoxin is the only cause of obesity, so this patient must have been noncompliant.
The liquid diet always fails unless initial weight is above 170 kg.
Endotoxin-producing bacteria are unrelated to fatty liver disease.

question_results.question: Which of the following would be the most accurate alternative title for the passage (paraphrased and not verbatim)?

"Gut Microbes May Play a Causal Role in Obesity"
"How to Lose Weight with Diet Drinks"
"The Complete Cure for Obesity Has Been Found"

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the researchers' claim that endotoxin-producing bacteria contributed to the patient's weight loss?
options: The patient's calorie intake dropped dramatically independent of the liquid diet composition.
Other patients with similar bacterial levels also lost weight on the same diet.
The patient's fatty liver improved as the bacterial levels fell.
The endotoxin is shown to activate a fat-producing gene in laboratory tests.

The general fear of summer weight exposure; American researchers' 2004 mice study; the holiday feasting reference.

The only diseases related to microbiota are digestive disorders.

distractor_results.question: If researchers wanted to strengthen the causal claim that the specific bacterium causes obesity, which study design change would best address Lita Proctor's concern mentioned in the passage?
options: Conduct a controlled experiment where subjects are randomly assigned to have their endotoxin-producing bacteria reduced and then compare weight changes to a placebo group.

distractor_results.question: Suppose a different obese patient had 10 percent of gut bacteria producing endotoxin and did not respond to the same liquid diet. Which explanation best aligns with the passage's information and logic?

question_results.question: Which of the following best captures the author's primary purpose in this passage?
options: To argue that online shopping should be banned because it is always unsafe
To warn readers about the risks of credit-card fraud online and suggest precautions
To describe the technical workings of secure internet protocols
To provide a history of CD Universe as a company

question_results.question: Suppose a company offers free goods but requires you to type your credit-card number on an unfamiliar site that shows https. Applying the passage’s guidance, what is the most consistent course of action?
options: Enter the card number because https guarantees absolute safety.
Avoid entering the card number and instead verify the site's reputation and security procedures first.
Immediately file a complaint with Mastercard before buying.
Share the offer widely on social media to get validation from others.

question_results.question: Which inference about the author’s attitude toward online shopping is best supported by the text?
options: The author is enthusiastic and encourages using any website for bargains.
The author is cautious and advises protective steps when shopping online.
The author believes online shopping is impossible to secure and should be avoided entirely.
The author thinks only physical stores can be trusted.

question_results.question: Which title best summarizes the overall message of the passage?
options: "Advances in Secure Web Protocol Design"
"How Retailers Handle Shipping Problems"
"Protecting Yourself from Online Credit-Card Fraud"
"The Rise of Online Music Retailers"

question_results.question: Which policy change would most strengthen the author's position that consumers can reduce online fraud risk without relying solely on corporate solutions?
options: Requiring all browsers to display an unchangeable security icon for https sites
Making customers fully liable for all fraudulent online charges
Promoting consumer education about recognizing secure sites, using phone verification when doubtful, and safeguarding passwords
Increasing credit limits for web-only cards

question_results.question: What unstated assumption underlies the author's suggestion to "keep your password safe"?
options: That passwords are irrelevant if a site uses https.
That unauthorized access to accounts is a likely way thieves use stolen information.
That passwords should be shared with customer service more frequently.
That remembering multiple passwords is unnecessary.

question_results.question: Which precaution does the passage explicitly recommend if you are unsure about a website's security?
options: Ask the retailer to send goods by registered mail
Give your credit-card information over the telephone
Only use cash payments
Use a different browser

Buying food guarantees the dog and pups will survive.

Appearance never affects whether someone receives help.

He was uninterested in the situation entirely.

Only people who look poor are trustworthy helpers.

options: Only those with means can provide meaningful help.

Public displays of charity are always performative and insincere.

question_results.question: What democratic action did citizens perform at the town meetings?
options: Wrote the state constitution
Elected the town's officials
Appointed federal judges
Declared war on neighboring towns

question_results.question: Where were town meetings usually held in New England towns?
options: In private homes
At the town hall in the central square
In church buildings
At the governor's office

question_results.question: If a modern city adopted the town meeting format described, which of the following consequences would most likely follow?
options: All governmental decisions would be handed over to national officials.
Citizens would gather periodically to debate local issues and choose local officials.
Town halls would be closed and replaced by courts.
Only property owners would regain exclusive voting rights.

question_results.question: Which combination of facts from the passage best supports the claim that town meetings influenced democratic practices beyond New England?
options: Town meetings were held at the town hall; only men voted in the eighteenth century.
Citizens elected officials at meetings; the town meeting idea has been imitated on radio, television, and by government agencies.
Meetings allowed everyone present to speak; town halls were centrally located.
Property owners originally voted; later only church members could vote.

question_results.question: Which statement best expresses the author's attitude toward town meetings?
options: The author views town meetings as an important democratic tradition worth preserving.
The author believes town meetings were harmful to democracy.
The author thinks town meetings were irrelevant to local governance.
The author is neutral and provides no indication of value.

question_results.question: Which phrase best paraphrases "express opinions freely" as used in the passage?
options: Be prevented from speaking
Share thoughts without restraint
Vote in secret ballots
Only listen to officials

question_results.question: Based on the passage, what is implied about voting rights over time in New England town meetings?
options: Voting rights became more inclusive as time passed.
Voting rights were more extensive in the eighteenth century than later.
Voting rights did not change and remained restricted.
Women were the only group given voting rights later.

question_results.question: How has the town meeting idea been used in recent years outside of towns?
options: Replaced by online polls only
Imitated as open sessions on radio and television and by government agencies
Abandoned entirely and forgotten
Used exclusively for corporate decision making

question_results.question: What inference can be drawn about the role of church membership in early town government?
options: Church membership had no influence on civic participation.
Church membership was a condition for active voting rights.
Only clergy could attend town meetings.
Church membership guaranteed automatic election to office.

question_results.question: Apply the passage's description to a hypothetical federal agency holding an open session: which outcome aligns best with the passage's depiction of town-meeting-style sessions?
options: The agency would refuse public input and make all decisions internally.
The session would provide an opportunity for free and open discussion on current problems and policies.
The agency would only allow property owners to speak.
The session would serve only as entertainment with no policy relevance.

question_results.question: What would be the most appropriate title for this passage?
options: "The Economic Policies of New England"
"Town Meetings: A Democratic Tradition from New England to Modern Media"
"Religious Practices in Eighteenth-Century America"
"How to Build a Town Hall"

question_results.question: Who originally voted in eighteenth-century town meetings?
options: All residents regardless of status
Only men who owned property and were church members
Only town officials
Women and children

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author's claim that the town meeting "became truly democratic"?
options: After expansion, many citizens were allowed to vote in town meetings.
Evidence showed that despite broader suffrage, a small elite still controlled outcomes through social pressure.
Town meetings were sometimes held outdoors in bad weather.
Radio programs occasionally broadcast town meeting discussions.

question_results.question: What purpose did the town meetings serve for citizens?
options: They were solely religious gatherings
To discuss local problems and express opinions freely
To host theatrical performances
To train new government officials

question_results.question: What unstated assumption underlies the passage's positive portrayal of town meetings?
options: Public participation and open discussion lead to better local governance.
Only elected officials should make community decisions behind closed doors.
Modern media are incapable of hosting public sessions.
Town halls must always be located in the central square.

question_results.question: Which phrase best paraphrases the government's view on the financial impact of over-drinking?
options: Alcohol misuse imposes a multi-billion-pound burden on the country.
Drinking has minimal economic consequences for Britain.
The government profits from alcohol sales across the UK.
Over-drinking reduces national spending on healthcare.

question_results.question: Which of these offers will be prohibited under the new rules?
options: "All you can drink for 10 pounds"
Discounted supermarket wine cases
Reduced-price cocktails during happy hour at supermarkets
Loyalty-card discounts for frequent supermarket shoppers

question_results.question: What does the mention of Paul Gascoigne's Euro '96 celebrations most likely serve to do in the passage?
options: Provide a well-known example that illustrates how the "dentist's chair" became publicized.
Argue that footballers are the main cause of Britain's drinking problems.
Claim that Euro '96 organizers promoted alcohol sales in supermarkets.
Suggest that all sporting events will now be banned from serving alcohol.

question_results.question: If a pub continued hosting speed drinking competitions after the new code, which combination of consequences mentioned in the text could it face?
options: Losing its license, a fine up to 20,000 pounds, or up to six months in prison.
Mandatory closure for one week and a public apology only.
A requirement to donate profits to health charities with no legal penalties.
Being forced to sell alcohol only during daytime hours.

question_results.question: Which of the following best captures the author's main purpose in the passage?
options: To report new regulatory measures aimed at curbing excessive drinking and note limitations in addressing wider causes.
To argue that supermarkets are entirely blameless and should be given awards for responsible selling.
To suggest that all alcohol consumption should be criminalized immediately.
To provide a historical account of drinking games in British culture.

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies critics' argument that the government "failed to use its most effective weapon"?
options: That increasing taxes or setting minimum prices on alcohol would reduce heavy drinking more effectively than banning pub promotions alone.
That banning drinking games is the single most effective method to stop under-age drinking.
That supermarkets can never be regulated by any government policy.
That publicans are responsible for creating alcohol taxes.

question_results.question: From which month will games that promote large consumption be banned?
options: April
January
September
December

question_results.question: Which piece of additional evidence would most weaken the passage's implied claim that supermarket cheap alcohol is a main source of Britain's drinking problems?
options: A nationwide study showing most under-age and excessive drinkers obtain alcohol primarily from friends and family, not supermarkets.
Testimony from a publican saying drinking games encourage responsible drinking.
An increase in supermarket sales of non-alcoholic beverages.
A report showing fines levied against pubs have decreased since the new code.

question_results.question: What will be banned in Britain this year to help prevent the heavy drinking culture?
options: Drinking games like the "dentist's chair"
Sale of alcohol in supermarkets
All pubs and clubs opening after midnight
Import of foreign alcoholic beverages

question_results.question: Who commented that the government and industry had a duty to act on heavy drinking?
options: Home Secretary Alan Johnson
A leading supermarket owner
The head of the alcohol vendors' association
A football player

question_results.question: What is implied as a major contributor to under-age and over-drinking despite the pub bans?
options: Cheap alcohol sold in supermarkets
Drinking games in private homes
Alcohol advertising on television
Higher taxes on alcohol

options: The new rules address promotional practices in pubs but leave pricing and taxation—key drivers of cheap alcohol availability—largely unchanged.
The passage claims the government has banned supermarket sales entirely, creating a gap in pub regulation.
The core gap is that pubs are required to provide free tap water but not soft drinks.
The policy gap is that drinking games are banned while sporting events are encouraged to serve free alcohol.

Pubs and clubs
Street vendors
Vending machines

options: Heavy drinking would likely remain a problem because cheaper supermarket alcohol would still encourage over-consumption despite the pub bans.
Heavy drinking would be eradicated because bans in pubs are sufficient on their own.
Pub attendance would increase dramatically and supermarkets would stop selling alcohol.
Alcohol-related costs to the country would immediately drop to zero.

question_results.question: What is one lesson the author says judo teaches beyond strength?
options: How to use weapons effectively
How to perform acrobatics
How to use the body wisely and leverage another person's body
How to avoid all contact with attackers

question_results.question: The author emphasizes practice when discussing judo. Which combined pieces of information from the text support the conclusion that judo training requires sustained effort?
options: The passage calls judo "muscle science" and notes that films show judo techniques.
The author says judo uses falls, holds, and throws, and that it "takes time and practice" to know judo.
The passage describes a woman holding an attacker and then mentions friends avoiding practice.
The text compares judo to being strong and watching television programs.

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author's argument that learning judo is useful for self-defense?
options: A study shows that people trained in judo are less likely to be injured in attacks.
Most real street attacks involve multiple attackers using weapons, making judo ineffective.
Judo teachers emphasize safe falling techniques.
Many television programs demonstrate judo successfully being used in staged scenarios.

question_results.question: According to the passage, what does "Jiu-jitsu" (as judo is sometimes called) mean?
options: Martial art of throwing
Muscle science
Self-defense technique
Body control

question_results.question: If the principle described in the passage (using an attacker's body against them) were applied to a business negotiation, which outcome best parallels the author's point?
options: Using greater financial resources to overpower the competitor.
Exploiting a competitor's weaknesses and momentum to reach a favorable deal despite having fewer resources.
Ignoring the competitor's strategy and relying on reputation alone.
Hiring more employees to outwork the opponent.

question_results.question: Based on the passage, what implicit assumption does the author make about strength versus technique?
options: Strength alone guarantees successful self-defense.
Technique and understanding of the body can overcome greater physical strength.
Only those who are naturally strong can learn judo.
Technique is useless without weapons.

question_results.question: The author contrasts being strong with "using the body wisely." Which conclusion follows from combining those ideas with the example of the woman in the film?
options: Physical strength is irrelevant and never helps in self-defense.
Knowledge of technique can allow a physically weaker person to subdue a stronger attacker.
Only women benefit from learning judo because men are always stronger.
Television programs are a substitute for real judo training.

question_results.question: Why does the author caution against practicing judo on friends?
options: Because judo is illegal to practice without a license.
Because friends might steal your judo equipment.
Because friends may get hurt if they cannot fall safely.
Because friends will always lose and feel embarrassed.

question_results.question: In the example from the film, how did the woman stop her attacker?
options: She ran away and called the police.
She used a gun to scare him off.
She threw him over her head and held him on the ground.
She negotiated with him until he left.

question_results.question: Which plausible real-world objection would the passage most likely NOT support?
options: Learning judo is useful because it teaches how to control an attacker.
Learning judo is risky to practice without proper instruction and safe falling techniques.
Learning judo is unnecessary because strength alone suffices in real attacks.
Learning judo can enable a weaker person to defend against a stronger one.

question_results.question: Which title would best summarize the passage's message without repeating exact phrasing from the text?
options: "Movie Violence and Urban Crime Rates"
"Why Judo Can Help You Survive an Attack"
"The History of Jiu-jitsu in Japan"
"Learning to Fall: A Film Review"

question_results.question: Which of the following best captures the author's primary purpose in the passage?
options: To describe the history of judo as a sport originating in Japan.
To argue that judo is unnecessary because films already show how to defend yourself.
To persuade readers that learning judo is useful for self-defense in a dangerous world.
To provide step-by-step instructions for judo techniques.

question_results.question: The author mentions seeing "a lot of violence of films" primarily to suggest that:
options: Films are an accurate training source for judo.
Exposure to on-screen violence replaces the need for real training.
Despite seeing violence on screen, people may lack real defensive skills.
Movies are the only reason crime figures rise.

question_results.question: Which sentence best reflects the author's view of the world at the start of the passage?
options: The world is peaceful and crime is decreasing.
The world is dangerous and crime figures keep rising.
The world is unpredictable but crime is stable.
The world is dangerous only because of films.

question_results.question: Which of the following statements best represents a logical unstated premise that supports the author's recommendation to learn judo?

question_results.question: Where did the narrator say he was when he was arrested?

In Richmond, a suburb of London

"How a Brief Arrest Became a Tale of Class and Privilege"
"A Detailed Guide to Arrest Procedures in Richmond"
"The Rise of Petty Theft in Suburban London"

question_results.question: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the narrator’s claim that his social background influenced the court’s dismissal?

options: Disturbing the peace
Vagrancy
Wandering with intent to commit an arrestable offence
Fraud

question_results.question: Which combination of facts from different parts of the text most strongly supports the narrator’s view that class and appearance mattered in his case?
options: He was nineteen and had long untidy hair; the policeman told his mother another youngster had been turned against the police.

He was looking for a job and had been strolling in the park; the police claimed there had been many petty thefts.
He had an English teacher ready as a witness and had left school recently; the police car arrived soon after.

question_results.question: What was the outcome of the court appearance at Richmond Magistrates' Court?
