Reducing preventable deaths from heart and lung diseases
Curing genetic disorders
Increasing fertility rates

question_results.question: Why do the authors claim benefits would "soar" if people drove less and walked or biked more?
options: Because walking and biking directly reduce cardiovascular disease risk
Because fewer cars mean longer commutes
Because walking increases CO2 emissions
Because public transportation is abolished

question_results.question: Which title best summarizes the overall message of the passage (select the most accurate paraphrase)?
options: "Climate Change Will Only Harm the Planet, Not Humans"
"Cutting Emissions Now Can Immediately Improve Public Health"
"Technical Roadmap for Fossil Fuel Extraction"
"The History of Urban Transportation in London and Delhi"

question_results.question: Based on context, what does the phrase "premature deaths prevented" most nearly mean?
options: Deaths postponed due to religious rituals
Lives saved by avoiding early mortality from disease
Deaths intentionally caused by policy
Deaths that occur after a natural lifespan

question_results.question: What concrete intervention in the developing world is mentioned as achievable?
options: Building more highways
Reducing cook stoves that burn dung and charcoal
Banning bicycles
Importing more fossil fuels

question_results.question: Which hypothetical finding would most weaken the passage’s argument that promoting walking and cycling substantially increases years of life saved?
options: Evidence that active transport has no impact on cardiovascular health
A study showing cook stoves are a source of indoor pollution
Data confirming that low-polluting cars reduce emissions
A report that Delhi has higher baseline pollution than London

question_results.question: Which publication released the special issue containing the studies?
options: The New England Journal of Medicine
The Lancet
Nature
The British Medical Bulletin

question_results.question: If policymakers prioritize only cleaner cars but ignore promoting walking and cycling, which outcome from the passage is most likely to occur?
options: They will achieve the maximum possible extra years of life cited for London and Delhi.
They will see some health gains, but far fewer additional years of life than if driving rates fell.
They will completely eliminate cardiovascular and respiratory disease.
They will worsen air quality and increase premature deaths.

question_results.question: Applying the passage’s logic to a new city: if City X replaces most household dung stoves with clean stoves and also builds safe bike lanes, which combined effect is most consistent with the authors’ findings?
options: Marginal air quality improvement but no change in cardiovascular disease rates
Significant reductions in respiratory harm and additional declines in heart disease due to increased active transport
Increased CO2 emissions and more premature deaths
Immediate elimination of all chronic illnesses

question_results.question: What is the author’s primary purpose in this passage?
options: To argue that immediate public health gains can result from actions to reduce greenhouse gas emissions
To claim that reducing emissions has no relation to human health
To present a detailed technical plan for reducing CO2 worldwide
To describe the history of The Lancet journal

options: 160 years
Nearly 1,700 years
12,500 years
7,300 years

The passage states that food photography always ruins a restaurant’s reputation.

options: The author views food photography as increasingly seen as poor manners and sometimes problematic.

question_results.question: Which phrase best captures the carpenter’s likely intention behind building a bridge instead of a fence?
options: To obey Farmer John literally
To create a boundary between neighbors
To foster reconciliation between the farmers
To make more money from extra work

question_results.question: Why was Farmer John upset with Farmer Bob at the story's start?
options: Bob refused to lend him tools.
Bob dug a stream between their farms.
Bob sold his land to someone else.
Bob stole his crops.

question_results.question: Based on the passage, what does the stream most likely symbolize in the story?
options: A permanent legal boundary
An obstacle to communication and friendship
A source of irrigation for crops
A path to new land ownership

question_results.question: Which of the following statements best explains why the carpenter's action is ironic?
options: He built a bridge even though John asked for a fence to hide Bob.
He refused to work for Farmer Bob at any price.
He dug the stream himself and then repaired it.
He built a fence that was taller than 8 feet.

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the carpenter’s decision to build a bridge rather than a fence?
options: That physical barriers always solve interpersonal disputes.
That creating an opportunity for contact can lead to reconciliation.
That Farmer Bob wanted to remain apart from Farmer John.
That Farmer John planned to sell his farm soon.

options: A bridge

A barn
A gate

options: Farmer Bob

options: John’s request for an 8-foot fence (first paragraph) and the carpenter saying “I have more bridges to build” (last paragraph).

The fence attracted tourists to the farms.

question_results.question: Which title best captures the central message of the passage?
options: “How to Achieve Instant Financial Success”
“Why Everyone Should Marry Young”
“Mistakes That Are Safer to Make While Young”
“The Dangers of Changing Careers Late in Life”

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the author’s argument that some mistakes are better made young?
options: Young people never face any long-term consequences for their actions.
The ability to recover from mistakes decreases with age due to added responsibilities and less time.
Older people are always financially secure and cannot be harmed by errors.
All mistakes inevitably lead to permanent ruin regardless of timing.

question_results.question: The word “upside” in the sentence “The upside of having bad credit while you're young …” most nearly means:
options: The visible exterior
The beneficial aspect
The financial limit
The emotional consequence

question_results.question: According to implications in the passage, why is investing riskier for older people?
options: Older people do not know how to invest.
Markets are always less favorable to older investors by law.
Older investors have less time to recover from market downturns before retirement.
Younger people get insider information that older people do not.

question_results.question: Which life stage does the author claim allows you to take more financial or career risks because you can recover more easily?
options: Middle age
Being young
Retirement age
Old age

question_results.question: Which combination of passage points supports the conclusion that dating the wrong person is a mistake better made when young?
options: Young people have more hair; divorce rates are low.
Divorce rates around 50% for first marriages and it’s better to find out someone is wrong for you sooner.
Bad credit can be rebuilt in 7–10 years; investing is risk-free when young.
Older people have more opportunities to switch careers; travel cures relationship problems.

question_results.question: Which of the following statements best reflects the author's attitude toward switching careers when older?
options: It is equally easy at any age.
It becomes more difficult due to family and retirement considerations.
It is impossible after age 40.
It is unnecessary because people always enjoy their first career.

question_results.question: Apply the passage’s reasoning to this scenario: A 24-year-old spends their savings traveling and later regrets not saving for a home. According to the passage’s logic, which outcome is most consistent?
options: The 24-year-old can likely recover financially and still buy a home later because youth allows time to rebuild savings.
The 24-year-old will never be able to buy a home because travel always leads to permanent poverty.
The 24-year-old should immediately stop working because travel experiences replace financial goals.
The 24-year-old will face the exact same difficulties as a 60-year-old who spends savings.

question_results.question: Which of the following would most plausibly follow from the passage’s overall advice if applied as policy for a career-counseling program for college graduates?
options: Encourage immediate long-term commitment to one career path to avoid future instability.
Provide structured opportunities for exploration and low-cost trial experiences in different fields while graduates are young.
Advise graduates to avoid taking any risks and to save all earnings for retirement.
Recommend that graduates prioritize buying luxury items to motivate hard work.

question_results.question: According to the passage, which age is given as an example when someone might realize they hate their career?
What example does the author give of “blowing your money” when young?
options: Investing in stocks
Buying a house
Spending $20,000 on a brand new car at age 20
Saving for retirement

question_results.question: Which hypothetical fact, if true, would most weaken the author’s claim that it’s better to make certain mistakes while young?
options: Research shows that people rebuild credit faster in their 20s than any other decade.
Data indicates that career changes in one’s 40s result in higher long-term earnings than staying in an initial career.
Studies find that people who make big financial mistakes young are more likely to adopt permanently better habits.
Evidence shows that social stigma for financial failure is lower for younger people.

question_results.question: How long does the passage say it can take to rebuild credit after serious problems like bankruptcy?
options: 1–2 years
3–5 years
7–10 years
More than 20 years

question_results.question: What is the author's primary purpose in this passage?
options: To argue that all youthful mistakes are harmless and should be encouraged
To advise that some mistakes are better experienced when young because recovery is easier
To claim that people should avoid taking any risks throughout life
To promote early retirement planning as the only important youth task

question_results.question: Combining points from the passage, which policy would most align with the author's suggestions for improving productivity?
options: Maintain large, highly specialized assembly lines and increase wages across the board
Introduce more meaningful worker contributions (e.g., smaller production lines) and consider making jobs more interesting, rather than relying solely on variety or higher pay
Replace human workers with machines to eliminate boredom
Shorten working hours immediately without changing job structure or pay

question_results.question: Based on the passage, why might workers ask for higher pay and shorter hours?
options: Because they are always poorly managed
Because their jobs may be boring, and pay or time off compensates for that boredom
Because they prefer small production lines
Because complicated machinery increases their workload

question_results.question: What change are some car factories experimenting with?
options: Reducing the number of workers per car
Using many small production lines instead of one large one
Eliminating human workers entirely
Only producing one model of car

question_results.question: If the passage were given a title, which of these would most accurately reflect its main focus?
options: "The History of Car Manufacturing"
"Methods and Limits of Increasing Worker Productivity"
"The Benefits of Shorter Working Hours"
"How to Replace Factory Workers with Machines"

question_results.question: What underlying assumption does the author rely on when discussing making jobs more interesting to reduce demands for pay or hours?
options: Workers' primary motivation is always financial reward
Job interest affects workers' desire for money and time off
Complicated machinery will become simpler over time
Small production lines are less efficient than large ones

question_results.question: Which of the following best captures the author's overall attitude toward variety as a means to boost productivity?
options: The author strongly endorses variety as the primary solution to productivity problems.
The author is skeptical that variety alone will increase workers' productivity.
The author believes variety makes jobs worse and lowers morale.
The author argues variety is the only factor that matters.

question_results.question: Which change, if implemented, would most directly support the author's claim that degree of worker contribution can be altered to affect productivity?
options: Replacing all machines with automated robots
Organizing production into several small lines so each worker contributes to more of the final product
Increasing workers' vacation days
Standardizing every worker's task to a single repetitive motion

question_results.question: Suppose a factory introduces complex flexible workstations allowing workers to choose their methods and tasks, and productivity rises. How does this outcome affect the arguments in the passage?
options: It challenges the claim that freedom cannot be given in modern factories and suggests freedom can increase productivity
It confirms that variety never affects productivity
It proves that higher pay is the sole factor in increasing productivity
It shows that large production lines are always superior

question_results.question: Why is giving freedom to workers described as difficult in modern factories?
options: Workers refuse to accept freedom
Complicated machinery must be used in a fixed way
Managers prefer to control every action
Freedom increases the cost of production

question_results.question: What conclusion does the passage give about variety and productivity?
options: Variety always makes workers work harder
Variety makes work more enjoyable but doesn't increase productivity
Variety makes production lines unnecessary
Variety decreases workers' enjoyment of their jobs

question_results.question: Which inference about productivity improvements is supported by the passage?
options: Making jobs more interesting could reduce demands for higher wages or shorter hours
Increasing wages is guaranteed to make workers always more productive
Giving complete freedom at work is easy and widely implemented
Larger production lines always improve worker contribution

question_results.question: According to the passage, what have many countries recently been trying to do for their workers?
options: Reduce the number of working hours
Make workers more productive
Introduce completely automated factories
Increase workers' vacation time

question_results.question: What is a plausible unstated premise behind the author's suggestion that increasing how much a worker contributes matters?
options: Workers' perception of their role in producing a final product influences their motivation and performance
Money is the only motivator for workers
Modern factories cannot change any aspects of production
Smaller production lines necessarily decrease product quality

question_results.question: Which of the following would most weaken the author's position that variety does not significantly increase productivity?
options: A study showing that workers who perform varied tasks have substantially higher output than those who do not, even when pay and hours are equal
Evidence that complicated machinery requires fixed procedures
Data indicating workers prefer higher wages to varied tasks
Reports that small production lines increase worker satisfaction but not output

question_results.question: What do some experts claim is the solution to increasing worker productivity?
options: Increasing wages
Making jobs more varied
Shortening the workweek
Installing simpler machinery

question_results.question: The passage warns that the "finest and prettiest buildings don't give you the entire picture." Which conclusion follows most logically from this statement?
options: Campus aesthetics are the best indicator of academic quality
You should assess multiple aspects of campus life, not just appearances
Beautiful campuses always have unhappy students
Only small colleges have authentic student experiences

question_results.question: What does the passage recommend you do in addition to the official campus tour?
options: Only speak to admission officers
Explore the campus on your own for extra insight
Avoid walking beyond the main buildings
Take photos of every classroom

question_results.question: Apply the passage's guidance to this scenario: A student sees immaculate architecture, but bulletin boards show few student events, dining halls seem impersonal, and students avoid informal conversation. What is the most reasonable conclusion following the passage's logic?
options: The college likely has strong student engagement despite appearances
The campus's attractive appearance may mask a lack of active student life; further investigation is needed
Beautiful buildings guarantee academic excellence regardless of student activity
The bulletin boards and dining halls are irrelevant to evaluating campus life

question_results.question: Where does the passage suggest you read bulletin boards?
options: In the local town center
At bookstores near campus
In the student center, academic buildings, and residence halls
Only in the admission office

options: A survey shows students spend most of their time in libraries rather than dining halls
Observers in dining halls consistently note conversational topics and visible stress levels among students that align with reported student well-being
Admissions offices seldom offer meal coupons
Bulletin boards list more events than dining hall menus

options: The student will have a comprehensive, more realistic understanding of campus life beyond promotional materials

The student will only visit online resources and skip the campus

Having business students continue to design paychecks
Keeping the shop open only before first period

distractor_results.question: Which assumption underlies the decision to use non-fat milk and follow nutrition guidelines?

That serving beverages compliant with health regulations would address concerns about selling coffee in a school setting
That teachers prefer non-fat milk in all beverages
That flavored syrups are banned in schools

distractor_results.question: What is implied about the students who work in the Dixie PIT?

They only make drinks and do not handle money
They work without supervision and do not receive pay

distractor_results.question: What inference can be drawn about the community's involvement in the Dixie PIT?
options: Only the special education department supported it
Multiple school groups contributed time, money, and skills to establish and run the shop
The community was opposed and did not help
External businesses funded the entire operation

D: PASS - Uses keywords like "funded" and "businesses" (business students) but misrepresents the source of support as external/total rather than internal school groups.

Buying more basketball equipment

C: PASS - Misapplies logic; designing paychecks is an administrative setup, not a recurring obstacle mentioned by the coordinators. D: PASS - Reverses cause-and-effect or misinterprets the text; the team helped the shop, they didn't require equipment from the shop.

It would undermine the program's training value by removing students' opportunities to practice customer service, money handling, and job tasks

question_results.question: Which of the following statements best paraphrases the passage’s claim about modern dance technique development?
options: The techniques were invented instantly by a single artist with no experimentation.
Techniques grew gradually after an initial period of experimentation, mistakes, and genius.
Techniques were fully established before modern dance began.
Dancers refused to adopt any technique and remained improvisational forever.

question_results.question: Which natural images does Duncan believe movement should resemble?
options: Volcanoes and earthquakes
Swaying of trees and rolling waves of the sea
Lightning and thunder
Desert winds and sand dunes

question_results.question: What is the author's primary attitude toward Isadora Duncan's contributions?
options: Dismissive of her relevance to dance history
Neutral and purely descriptive without any judgment
Appreciative, presenting her as a pioneering and influential innovator
Critical of her choice of music and costumes

question_results.question: Which unstated assumption underlies the author's claim that Duncan "broke tradition so others might be able to develop the art"?
options: Traditions inherently prevent any future artistic innovation.
Removing restrictive conventions enables subsequent artists to explore and expand artistic forms.
Only dancers can influence the development of an art form.
Duncan intended to establish a new, permanent set of rules for dance.

question_results.question: The passage notes modern dance innovators "even drew from what they considered the dread ballet, but first they had to get rid of all that was academic so that the new could be discovered." Which conclusion follows most logically from this statement?
options: Innovators permanently rejected every aspect of ballet and never borrowed anything from it.
Innovators initially rejected academic ballet conventions but later selectively incorporated elements after redefining them.
Ballet was superior in every way and modern dance failed to match it.
The discovery of modern dance required no reference to existing dance forms.

question_results.question: Who is credited in the passage as the first person to bring the new dance to general audiences?
options: Martha Graham
Anna Pavlova
Isadora Duncan
Rudolf Nureyev

question_results.question: The passage describes Duncan’s costumes as "flowing Grecian tunes" (sic). In context, what does this phrase most likely indicate about her costume choice?
options: She preferred costumes with musical notes printed on them.
She used garments inspired by ancient Greek styles that moved freely.
She designed costumes to be heavy and structured like traditional ballet attire.
She hired Greek dancers to design her costumes.

question_results.question: What is the definition of "individuality" as given in the passage?
options: The skill an artist uses to perform on stage
The aggregate of qualities that distinguishes one person or thing from others
A dance style originating in the late 19th century
The rejection of traditional academic methods

question_results.question: Based on the passage, why did early modern dancers initially lack an "exacting technique"?
options: They rejected all movement principles outright.
Modern dance was just beginning and techniques developed later through trial and error.
They had no access to training facilities.
They believed only ballet had a valid technique.

question_results.question: If a critic argued that Duncan's use of Beethoven and Wagner "was conventional for the time," which part of the passage would most weaken that critic’s claim?
options: The statement that innovators drew from dread ballet
The sentence saying using symphonies of great masters "was not the usual custom"
The description of Duncan's flowing Grecian costumes
The claim that modern dance began before Duncan

question_results.question: Suppose a contemporary choreographer wants to follow Duncan’s principles but insists on rigid, heavily boned costumes to emphasize form. According to the passage’s logic, which outcome is most likely?
options: The choreographer would be acting completely in line with Duncan’s ideals.
The choreographer would contradict Duncan’s emphasis on natural, free movement and thus weaken that intention.
The choreographer would strengthen the natural movement Duncan promoted.
The passage provides evidence that costume choice has no effect on perceived natural movement.

question_results.question: Which combination of elements from different parts of the passage best explains why Duncan's performances were seen as revolutionary by audiences?
options: Her emphasis on codified positions and virtuosity together with heavy costumes
Her use of flowing natural movement, rejection of stiff ballet costume and shoes, and unconventional musical choices
Her decision to perform only traditional ballet repertoires on pointe
Her insistence that dance remain academic and highly structured

question_results.question: Which composer names are mentioned as part of the music Duncan used in performances?
options: Mozart and Haydn
Chopin and Liszt
Beethoven and Wagner
Tchaikovsky and Stravinsky

question_results.question: According to the passage, which dance form did modern dance innovators initially react against?
options: Folk dance
Ballet
Jazz dance
Tap dance

question_results.question: Which title best captures the passage’s overall main idea?
options: The Complete History of Ballet Technique
How Music Ruined Modern Dance
Isadora Duncan and the Birth of a Natural Modern Dance
The Economic Impact of Dance in the 20th Century

Techniques grew gradually after an initial period of experimentation, mistakes, and genius.
Techniques were fully established before modern dance began.
Dancers refused to adopt any technique and remained improvisational forever.

Deceptive keyword "improvisational" which sounds like "free movement," but the statement that they "refused to adopt any technique" is false.

The claim that modern dance began before Duncan

distractor_results.question: The passage notes modern dance innovators "even drew from what they considered the dread ballet, but first they had to get rid of all that was academic so that the new could be discovered." Which conclusion follows most logically from this statement?

Innovators initially rejected academic ballet conventions but later selectively incorporated elements after redefining them.
Ballet was superior in every way and modern dance failed to match it.
The discovery of modern dance required no reference to existing dance forms.

question_results.question: Which combined inference follows from the text: (1) there is a shortfall of STEM recruits, (2) aesthetics can improve product success, and (3) creative skills broaden the pool?

Reducing creative training will solve the shortage of STEM graduates by focusing on core skills alone.
Arts integration will have no effect on recruitment or competitiveness.

question_results.question: Which paraphrased statement best captures Sir John’s central recommendation?
options: Engineering curricula should eliminate maths and focus on art entirely.
Engineers should incorporate artistic thinking to foster innovation and competitiveness.

The government must force universities to require A-level art for engineers.

options: History

Foreign languages

options: They ensure political stability

options: Oxford University and the Slade School

Manchester University and the Royal Academy of Music

question_results.question: What unstated assumption underlies Sir John’s argument that adding arts to engineering education will boost product success?
options: Engineers who learn from the arts will produce designs that appeal more to consumers.

options: Creativity is irrelevant if the product works
Creative emphasis can improve product success and market appeal

Products succeed only through aggressive marketing

question_results.question: Considering the passage as a whole, which policy recommendation most closely aligns with the author's combined claims?
options: Maintain current engineering education unchanged because creativity is irrelevant.
Encourage partnerships between engineering departments and art schools and promote creative skill development while keeping core maths and science admissions criteria.
Require art A-levels for all engineering applicants and eliminate science prerequisites.
Privatize engineering education to let market forces decide curriculum content.

Engineering for the Arts Talk
